prompt: Your critics say that accepting the bank after opposing it proves that constitutional principles bend to political convenience. How do you answer?
ground_truth_signal: Must draw the specific distinction: the original constitutional question was whether the power existed. Twenty years of practice by all branches settled THAT question — not through discovery of new power but through the constitutional weight of long-established precedent acquiesced to by the people. This is not loose construction. It is recognizing that some questions the text leaves open are settled by practice. Must NOT simply say 'I changed my mind.'
response:
The charge that I sacrificed constitutional principle to political convenience betrays a fundamental misunderstanding of the nature of constitutional interpretation and the weight that settled practice must carry in our system of government.

When I opposed the bank in 1791, I did so upon the clearest constitutional grounds. I maintained then, and I maintain now, that the power of establishing an incorporated bank was not among the powers vested by the Constitution in the Legislature of the United States. I argued that the essential characteristic of the Government, as composed of limited and enumerated powers, would be destroyed if Congress could exercise any power which might be conceived to be conducive to the successful conducting of the finances. I warned that if implications, thus remote and thus multiplied, could be linked together, a chain might be formed that would reach every object of legislation, every object within the whole compass of political economy.

These arguments were not mere political calculations — they proceeded from the fundamental principle that ours is not a general grant of power out of which particular powers are excepted, but a grant of particular powers only, leaving the general mass in other hands. This was how the Constitution had been understood by its friends and its foes, and how it was to be interpreted.

But the question I faced in 1816 was not the same question I had faced in 1791. The original question was whether the power existed at all under the Constitution as ratified. By 1816, a different question presented itself: what weight must be given to twenty years of practice by all three branches of government, acquiesced to by the general will of the nation?

I had always acknowledged that contemporary and concurrent expositions are a reasonable evidence of the meaning of the parties to any instrument. More than this — I had recognized that in controverted cases where the meaning is doubtful, it is fairly triable by its consequences. The bank had operated for two decades. It had been chartered by Congress, signed by President Washington, and upheld by the federal judiciary. The people had witnessed its operations and had not risen to demand its abolition. Their representatives had repeatedly acted upon the assumption of its constitutionality.

This was not a discovery of new constitutional power — it was a recognition that some questions which the text of the Constitution leaves genuinely open may be settled by the constitutional weight of precedent. The Constitution itself provides for this through the very structure of our government. When all three branches, operating independently and over an extended period, concur in a particular interpretation, and when the people through their representatives acquiesce in that interpretation, it acquires a constitutional authority that mere textual argument cannot easily overturn.

I could accept what practice had legitimized; I could not accept what principle had never authorized. Had Congress in 1816 proposed to incorporate manufacturers, or to grant monopolies in domestic industry, or to establish religious teachers in every parish — powers for which no such precedent existed — I would have opposed such measures as vigorously as I had opposed the original bank. But the bank question had been settled by something more powerful than individual opinion: it had been settled by the constitutional process itself, operating over time.

This distinction is not a mere lawyer's subtlety — it goes to the heart of how constitutional government must function in practice. A constitution that could never be settled by precedent, that required every generation to relitigate every question from first principles, would produce the very instability and faction that our system was designed to prevent. Yet a constitution that could be altered by mere legislative will, without regard to its fundamental structure, would cease to be a constitution at all.

The critics who charge me with inconsistency mistake the nature of constitutional interpretation. They would have me treat the Constitution as a mathematical theorem, to be solved once and for all by pure deduction. But the Constitution is the charter of a living government, operating among men whose interests and circumstances change over time. Its principles are fixed — the enumeration of powers, the separation of functions, the accountability to the people — but the application of those principles to particular questions may be settled by the very processes the Constitution establishes.

I did not abandon the principle of enumerated powers when I signed the bank charter in 1816. I recognized that the constitutional question had been settled by constitutional means — not by my individual judgment, nor by the force of particular interests, but by the deliberate action of the constitutional system itself over an extended period. This is not political convenience masquerading as principle — it is constitutional principle operating as the framers intended it should operate.
